text.title: java-spring-async Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for java-spring-async. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Projects MUST define at least one named `ThreadPoolTaskExecutor` bean (e.g., "emailExecutor", "taskExecutor") via `@Configuration`. The executor MUST set `RejectedExecutionHandler` to `CallerRunsPolicy` for backpressure. Core pool size MUST be calculated as cores × (1 + wait/compute ratio), with sensible defaults (4 for email, 8 for general tasks). Graceful shutdown MUST be enabled: `setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true)` and `setAwaitTerminationSeconds(30)`. Every `@Async` method MUST explicitly name its executor: `@Async("emailExecutor")`. Omitting the name falls back to Spring's default, which changes between Boot versions.
rule.rationale: Named executors prevent contention and allow independent tuning. `CallerRunsPolicy` provides backpressure when the queue is full, preventing unbounded memory growth. Pool sizing prevents thread explosion. Graceful shutdown ensures in-flight tasks complete before the app stops. Explicit naming makes executor choice visible in the code and stable across versions.

rule.statement: Every `@Async` method MUST return `CompletableFuture<T>` (not `Future<T>`, not `void`). The method body MUST wrap the computation in a try-catch or use `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync()` to ensure exceptions reach the caller. Callers MUST NOT assume fire-and-forget; they MUST `.get()` or `.thenAccept()` to observe completion and errors. If fire-and-forget is truly desired, the error handling MUST be explicit (logged, sent to Sentry, etc.).
rule.rationale: `CompletableFuture<T>` allows callers to compose (`.thenCombine()`, `.allOf()`) and observe exceptions. `void` methods swallow exceptions unless an `AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler` is registered, and even then, only uncaught exceptions — caught exceptions inside the method vanish silently. `Future<T>` is older API; `CompletableFuture` is composable and more flexible.

rule.statement: ThreadPoolTaskExecutor MUST use a `TaskDecorator` to capture and restore MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context, used for request-id) and `SecurityContext` (authentication principal). Without a decorator, logs in the async thread lose correlation with the original request, and authenticated endpoints called inside async methods throw `AuthenticationException`.
rule.rationale: Request tracing relies on request-id propagation via MDC. Async threads starting fresh have empty MDC, breaking correlation. Security operations inside async methods need the authenticated principal; `SecurityContextHolder` is ThreadLocal and empty in a new thread. The decorator pattern is the standard way to propagate context.

rule.statement: CRITICAL: Do NOT call an `@Async` method from the same bean (self-invocation). Spring proxies only wrap external calls; self-invocation bypasses the proxy and runs synchronously. If self-invocation is necessary, inject the bean into itself or use `AopContext.currentProxy()`. Code review MUST flag any self-invocation. No `@Async` method inside `@Transactional` — the outer transaction does not propagate to the async thread; rollback is not coordinated. If async work is conditional on a transaction, move the `@Async` call outside the transaction boundary.
rule.rationale: Self-invocation is a well-known Spring AOP gotcha. The caller gets back the result immediately (blocking), defeating the purpose of `@Async`. The proxy pattern requires external method calls. `@Transactional` with `@Async` is even more insidious: the caller thinks the async work is within the transaction boundary, but it runs in a new thread with a new transaction (if any), silently causing data corruption on partial failure.
